_notes.purpose: IFS wiki articles for in-app reference. Content is Obsidian-flavored markdown. Wikilinks use [[page_id]] syntax — resolve to page id values. Updatable: add pages, edit content, or reorder without code changes.

pages.title: Welcome to the IFS Wiki
pages.content:
# Welcome to the IFS Wiki

This wiki is your reference companion for everything in the app — the concepts behind it, the language it uses, and how each feature works. Whether you're brand new to Internal Family Systems or deepening an existing practice, these pages are here to support you.

---

## How to Use This Wiki

Pages are organized in three tiers. You don't need to read them in order — follow what's relevant to where you are.

**Start here if you're new:**
- [[01_What_Is_IFS]] — What is Internal Family Systems?
- [[02_What_Are_Parts]] — The core premise
- [[03_The_Self]] — The calm center of your inner world

**Go deeper when you're ready:**
- [[04_How_the_System_Works]] — Protectors, exiles, and the inner logic of protection
- [[05_Managers]] — Parts that try to keep things under control
- [[06_Firefighters]] — Parts that respond to overwhelm
- [[07_Exiles]] — Parts carrying old wounds

**Understand the app:**
- [[08_Your_Parts_Map]] — What the map shows and how to read it
- [[09_Assessments]] — How the three-tier assessment sequence works
- [[10_Refine]] — Making your parts truly your own
- [[11_Elaboration]] — Going deeper with a single part
- [[12_Trailhead]] — Following activation chains to their source
- [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In]] — Tracking your access to Self
- [[14_Update]] — Logging insights and changes over time
- [[15_Couples_Connection]] — Bringing IFS into relationship

---

## Key Concepts at a Glance

| Term | Plain meaning |
|---|---|
| Part | A distinct inner voice, feeling, or pattern of behavior |
| Self | Your core presence — calm, curious, and compassionate |
| Manager | A part that prevents pain before it happens |
| Firefighter | A part that reacts when pain breaks through anyway |
| Exile | A part carrying old hurt, fear, or shame |
| Blending | When a part's energy merges with yours and feels like "you" |
| Unblending | Creating space between you (Self) and a part |
| Trailhead | Any thought, feeling, sensation, or impulse that leads inward |
| Unburdening | The process of releasing old pain a part has been carrying |
| Self-leadership | Living from your core Self rather than from reactive parts |

---

> **A note on language:** IFS uses the word "parts" instead of emotions, impulses, or inner voices — not because it's clinical jargon, but because it honors that each of these experiences has its own logic, history, and intention. Getting familiar with the language opens up a different kind of self-understanding.

---

*This wiki lives inside the app and grows alongside your work. Pages link to each other — follow what draws your curiosity.*

pages.title: What Is IFS?
pages.content:
# What Is IFS?

**Internal Family Systems (IFS)** is a model of the mind developed by psychologist Dr. Richard Schwartz. Its central insight is simple but profound: the mind is not a single unified thing. It's a system — more like a family of inner voices, feelings, and patterns than a single "you."

---

## The Basic Idea

Most of us have had the experience of being "in two minds" about something — part of you wants to go, part of you wants to stay. Part of you feels confident; another part doubts everything. IFS takes this seriously. Rather than treating these inner conflicts as noise to be managed, it treats them as real — as distinct *parts* of your inner world, each with its own history, perspective, and intention.

The goal of IFS isn't to silence or overpower any part. It's to understand them, help them feel safe, and ultimately lead from your deepest, most grounded self.

---

## Why "Family Systems"?

Schwartz noticed that the dynamics between inner parts often mirror the dynamics in actual families — protective parts that cover for vulnerable ones, parts that argue or fight for control, parts that go quiet and hide. The "family systems" framing helps explain why working with one part often affects the whole.

Just as healing in a family involves listening, understanding roles, and building trust — not forcing compliance — the same is true inside.

---

## What Makes IFS Different

Many approaches to mental health focus on managing or reducing symptoms — quieting the inner critic, suppressing anxiety, challenging negative thoughts. IFS starts from a different premise:

> Every part, no matter how disruptive or painful, developed for a good reason. It had a job to do. Understanding that job — and what the part was originally protecting — changes everything.

This doesn't mean endorsing harmful behaviors. It means recognizing that behind every firefighter binge, every perfectionist spiral, every shutdown, there's a part that learned this strategy for a reason — and that part can change when it no longer needs to work so hard.

---

## The Three-Part Structure

IFS organizes inner parts into three broad roles:

- **[[05_Managers]]** — proactive protectors that try to prevent pain, maintain control, and keep exiles hidden
- **[[06_Firefighters]]** — reactive protectors that respond when pain breaks through anyway
- **[[07_Exiles]]** — younger, more vulnerable parts carrying old wounds, fears, and beliefs

At the center of all of it is the **[[03_The_Self]]** — not a part, but the aware, compassionate presence that can relate to all parts without becoming them.

---

## IFS and This App

This app is built on IFS principles. The [[09_Assessments|assessment sequence]] helps you discover which parts are active in your system. The [[08_Your_Parts_Map|parts map]] visualizes how they relate. Features like [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]], [[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]], and [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In|Self-Energy Check-In]] guide you through the actual work of getting to know your parts and strengthening your access to Self.

You don't need to be in therapy to use this app — but if you are, it's designed to support and extend that work.

---

**→ Continue to:** [[02_What_Are_Parts]]
**→ Or jump to:** [[03_The_Self]] | [[04_How_the_System_Works]]

pages.title: What Are Parts?
pages.content:
# What Are Parts?

A **part** is any distinct inner experience that has its own voice, feeling, perspective, or pattern — and that shows up somewhat consistently across different situations in your life.

You might recognize a part as:
- A voice that criticizes you when you make a mistake
- A feeling of dread before social situations
- An impulse to check your phone when things get uncomfortable
- A sense of needing to be perfect or nothing at all
- A sudden numbness when someone gets too close

Parts aren't problems. They're not signs that something is wrong with you. They're internal actors — and like any actor, each one learned its role for a reason.

---

## Parts Are Not Just Emotions

It's tempting to think of parts as simply emotions — anxiety, anger, sadness. But parts are more than feelings. A part has:

- **A role** — something it's trying to do or prevent
- **A perspective** — beliefs it holds about you, others, or the world
- **A history** — it usually developed at a specific time, in response to specific experiences
- **Physical sensations** — parts often live in the body as tension, tightness, heat, or collapse
- **An intention** — even parts that cause suffering are usually trying to protect something

When you approach a part with curiosity — "what are you trying to do for me?" — the answer is almost always more coherent than you'd expect.

---

## Multiplicity Is Normal

IFS holds that having many parts isn't a disorder — it's how minds work. Everyone has an internal system. The difference between a system that functions well and one that causes suffering usually comes down to two things:

1. How burdened the parts are (how much old pain they're still carrying)
2. How much access there is to [[03_The_Self|Self]] — the calm, curious center that can actually lead the system

When parts are under a lot of pressure, or when Self is consistently crowded out, the internal system can become loud, reactive, and hard to navigate. That's what this app is designed to help with.

---

## How Parts Form

Most parts formed in response to experiences — often early ones — where something needed to be managed, suppressed, or survived. A child who learned that expressing anger led to rejection might develop a part that suppresses anger. A child who discovered that being perfect got them approval might grow a perfectionist part that still runs the same strategy decades later.

The part formed because it worked — or at least helped at the time. The problem is that many parts keep running old strategies long after those strategies are necessary. They haven't had a chance to update.

---

## The Three Roles

IFS groups parts into three functional categories based on their role in the system:

**[[05_Managers]]** work proactively — they try to maintain control, prevent pain, and keep things running smoothly before anything goes wrong. They often show up as inner critics, planners, people-pleasers, or hypervigilant monitors.

**[[06_Firefighters]]** are reactive — they activate when a manager fails and overwhelming feelings start to break through. They'll do almost anything to stop the pain: distract, numb, rage, binge, dissociate.

**[[07_Exiles]]** are the parts being protected. They carry old wounds — feelings of shame, abandonment, not being enough, or not being safe. Because their pain is so intense, the system works hard to keep them out of awareness.

---

## Recognizing Your Parts

You've already been given a [[09_Assessments|personalized map of your parts]] through the assessment process. As you use the app, you'll get to know them more specifically — their triggers, their physical signatures, what they're protecting, and what they need.

The work isn't to become part-free. It's to build a different kind of relationship with your parts — one where you're genuinely leading, and they're not.

---

**→ Continue to:** [[03_The_Self]]
**→ Or explore:** [[05_Managers]] | [[06_Firefighters]] | [[07_Exiles]]

pages.title: The Self
pages.content:
# The Self

In IFS, **the Self** isn't a part — it's the aware presence underneath all parts. It's what's left when parts aren't blended with you, activated, or running the show. And according to IFS, it's always there. It can't be destroyed, only obscured.

The Self is the part of you that can hold everything — all your parts, all their pain, all their competing agendas — without being overwhelmed, without taking sides, and without losing perspective.

---

## The 8 C's of Self

When you're in Self, you tend to experience some or all of these qualities:

| Quality | What it feels like |
|---|---|
| **Calm** | A settled, grounded presence — not empty, but steady |
| **Curious** | Genuine interest in understanding parts, even difficult ones |
| **Compassion** | Warmth toward all parts, including ones that cause harm |
| **Clarity** | The ability to see the bigger picture without being caught in it |
| **Confidence** | Trust in your capacity to handle whatever arises |
| **Creativity** | Flexibility, resourcefulness, the ability to find new approaches |
| **Courage** | Willingness to face difficult parts, feelings, or truths |
| **Connectedness** | Feeling in touch with yourself, others, and something larger |

You don't need all eight at once. Even a thread of one — a moment of genuine curiosity toward an anxious part — is enough to begin changing the relationship.

---

## The 5 P's

Some practitioners also describe Self through five additional qualities — particularly useful for recognizing Self-presence in action:

- **Presence** — being fully here, not pulled into the past or future
- **Patience** — willing to move at the pace of the parts
- **Perspective** — holding the long view
- **Persistence** — staying engaged even when it's hard
- **Playfulness** — a lightness that makes even difficult work feel possible

---

## Self Is Not a State You Achieve

One of the most important things to understand about Self: it isn't a destination. You don't "become" the Self and stay there. Parts will activate, blend, take over. That's normal. The practice is noticing when that's happened — and finding your way back.

The [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In|Self-Energy Check-In]] in the app is designed to support exactly this: tracking your access to Self over time, noticing what helps and what diminishes it, and developing practices to return to it more readily.

---

## Blending and Unblending

**Blending** is when a part's energy merges with yours so completely that you experience yourself *as* that part. You don't feel angry — you feel like you *are* anger. You don't feel the perfectionist's anxiety — you feel like you genuinely *are* someone who needs everything to be perfect.

Blending isn't a failure. It's inevitable. The system is designed this way — parts blend when they feel they need to.

**Unblending** is the practice of creating space. Not pushing a part away, but gently differentiating: *this is a part I'm experiencing, not the whole of who I am.* Often it's as simple as noticing: "There's a part of me that feels…" That small shift in language — "a part of me" rather than "I" — begins the unblending.

The app supports unblending through [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]], [[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]], and the [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In|Self-Energy Check-In]].

---

## Self-Leadership

**Self-leadership** is the long arc of IFS work — moving toward a life where the Self is genuinely in charge. Not suppressing or eliminating parts, but leading them. Parts that have been heard, understood, and unburdened naturally step back and allow Self to lead.

In a Self-led system:
- Parts are present, but don't run the show
- You can choose your response rather than react automatically
- Inner conflict decreases because parts trust the Self to lead
- Even difficult feelings can be held with curiosity rather than overwhelm

This isn't a permanent achievement — it's an ongoing relationship. And it starts with the simplest possible step: getting curious about what's happening inside.

---

**→ Continue to:** [[04_How_the_System_Works]]
**→ Or explore:** [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In]] | [[02_What_Are_Parts]]

pages.title: How the System Works
pages.content:
# How the System Works

Your inner system has a logic to it — even when it feels chaotic, reactive, or hard to understand. IFS offers a map for that logic. Once you see the structure, a lot of behavior that used to feel random or shameful starts to make sense.

---

## The Basic Architecture

At the foundation of every inner system is a set of **[[07_Exiles|Exiles]]** — vulnerable parts carrying old wounds. These parts typically developed early in life, in response to experiences of abandonment, rejection, shame, loss, or feeling unsafe or unloved.

Exiles carry pain the system couldn't process at the time — and because their feelings are so intense, the rest of the system learned to protect against them.

That protection takes two forms:

**[[05_Managers]]** work *proactively*. Before an exile's feelings can surface, managers try to keep things under control — through perfectionism, people-pleasing, hypervigilance, emotional suppression, caretaking, or constant planning. Their job is prevention.

**[[06_Firefighters]]** work *reactively*. When exile feelings break through anyway — when something triggers that old wound and the managers couldn't hold it — firefighters respond with emergency tactics: dissociation, substance use, rage, bingeing, self-harm, impulsive behavior. Their job is emergency containment.

**[[03_The_Self]]** is at the center — the aware, compassionate presence that can relate to all parts without becoming them. When Self is leading, parts can relax. When parts are running the show, Self gets crowded out.

---

## Why the System Protects So Hard

It helps to understand what exiles actually carry. It's not just sadness or anxiety — exiles carry *beliefs*. Core beliefs about who you are and what the world is like:

- *I'm not enough*
- *I'm fundamentally flawed*
- *I'm not safe*
- *I will be abandoned if people see the real me*
- *I have to earn love*

These beliefs formed in specific moments — a critical parent, an absence that felt like abandonment, a time you were humiliated or helpless. They made sense in context. But exiles don't know time has passed. They're still living in that moment, and the whole protective system formed around making sure those feelings don't surface.

When you understand this, the manager who won't stop criticizing you, or the firefighter who drinks too much — they start to look less like enemies and more like parts doing an exhausting job they never asked for.

---

## Protection Chains

Parts rarely operate alone. A common pattern is a **protection chain** — where one activation leads to another:

> A trigger activates an exile's pain → a manager tries to suppress it (criticism, control) → exile pain breaks through anyway → a firefighter responds (drinking, rage, dissociation)

The [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] feature is designed specifically to help you follow these chains — starting with whatever you notice first (a feeling, a sensation, an impulse) and tracing it back through the protection system to its source.

---

## Polarizations

Sometimes two parts are locked in direct conflict — each one activating the other in a loop. These are called **polarizations**.

Common examples:
- A part that wants to pursue a goal ↔ a part that procrastinates
- A part that wants connection ↔ a part that fears intimacy and pulls back
- A critic that drives high standards ↔ an inner rebel that resists being pushed

Polarized parts often exhaust the system — each one amplifying the other's response. Working with polarizations usually involves helping both parts understand that they're actually trying to protect the same exile, just with opposite strategies.

---

## Coalitions

Not all part relationships are conflicts. Parts also form **coalitions** — alliances where they reinforce each other. A Perfectionist and a Critic might work together, each supporting the other's goal of keeping standards high and exile pain at bay.

The [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]] makes these coalitions visible — you'll see them as connected parts on the relationship web.

---

## What Changes Things

The protective system doesn't change through force. You can't argue a manager out of its role or willpower a firefighter into retiring. What changes the system is:

1. **Developing trust with protectors** — helping them understand that the Self can now handle what they've been protecting against
2. **Healing the exile** — when an exile's burden is lifted, protectors no longer have a reason to work so hard
3. **Self-leadership** — the more consistently the Self is present, the more parts begin to relax their grip

This is the arc of IFS work. And every feature in the app is oriented toward this arc.

---

**→ Explore part types:** [[05_Managers]] | [[06_Firefighters]] | [[07_Exiles]]
**→ Explore app features:** [[12_Trailhead]] | [[08_Your_Parts_Map]]

pages.title: Managers
pages.content:
# Managers

**Managers** are proactive protective parts. Their job is to maintain control, keep things running smoothly, and prevent the pain of [[07_Exiles|exiles]] from surfacing — before anything has a chance to go wrong.

If [[06_Firefighters|Firefighters]] are the emergency response team, Managers are preventive medicine. They work constantly in the background, monitoring, planning, adjusting, and keeping the inner world stable enough that exile feelings never need to emerge.

---

## What Managers Do

The specific strategies managers use vary widely, but they all share a common goal: *control the environment, the self, or the relationship well enough that nothing threatening can happen.*

Common manager strategies include:
- Setting impossibly high standards (perfectionism)
- Monitoring what others think (hypervigilance, comparing)
- Taking care of others before yourself (caretaking, pleasing)
- Suppressing or minimizing feelings (intellectualizing, rationalizing)
- Staying one step ahead of everything (planning, analyzing)
- Restricting spontaneous impulses (controlling, rule-keeping)
- Avoiding conflict at all costs (peacemaking, minimizing)

From the outside — and sometimes from the inside — manager behavior can look like a personality trait or a virtue. The perfectionist looks diligent. The caretaker looks generous. The planner looks responsible. And in many ways, they are all of these things. The problem isn't the strategy itself; it's how much energy it takes to run it constantly, and what gets suppressed in the process.

---

## The Hidden Cost

Managers work hard. And because their job is prevention — stopping something from happening — they often can't fully relax. There's always another thing to control, another potential failure to preempt, another person whose approval needs to be secured.

This creates a particular kind of exhaustion: not the exhaustion of having done too much, but of perpetually monitoring and bracing for what might happen.

Managers also tend to be hard on the self. Many of the inner critic, self-judgment, and shame-inducing voices in a system are manager parts running protection — keeping you small, quiet, and compliant so that nothing risky gets tried and nothing painful gets exposed.

---

## Manager Types in the App

The app's assessment identifies 14 distinct manager types across four primary orientations:

**Cognitive-primary managers** operate mainly through thought patterns — analyzing, planning, comparing, doubting, or intellectualizing as ways to maintain control.

**Emotional-primary managers** manage the inner world by monitoring and regulating feeling — pleasing others, minimizing your own emotions, keeping the peace, or maintaining relentless optimism.

**Interoceptive-primary managers** work through the body — staying physically tense and ready, scanning for bodily danger signals, or suppressing physical sensation.

**Impulse-primary managers** maintain control by restricting behavior — adhering to rigid rules, restraining natural responses, managing time obsessively, or keeping spontaneity in check.

---

## Relating to Managers

The first instinct many people have when they identify a manager is frustration — *this part has been running my life.* That reaction is understandable. But in IFS, the approach is curiosity, not confrontation.

The question to ask a manager isn't "why do you do this?" with accusation — it's genuinely: *what are you afraid would happen if you didn't?*

The answer is almost always that the manager is protecting something — an exile whose pain it's convinced will be unbearable. Most managers have been doing this job for a long time and don't yet trust that the Self can handle what they've been containing. Building that trust is part of the work.

---

## Managers and the App

- Your identified manager parts appear on your [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]], connected to the exiles they protect and the firefighters they work alongside.
- The [[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]] feature lets you go deeper with a specific manager — exploring its history, its fears, what it's protecting, and what it might need.
- The [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] feature often begins with a manager's activation — and traces back through the protection chain to the exile underneath.
- The [[10_Refine|Refine]] feature lets you personalize a manager with a name and appearance that resonates with your actual experience of it.

---

**→ See also:** [[06_Firefighters]] | [[07_Exiles]] | [[04_How_the_System_Works]]

pages.title: Firefighters
pages.content:
# Firefighters

**Firefighters** are reactive protective parts. Unlike [[05_Managers|Managers]], who try to prevent pain before it surfaces, Firefighters respond *after* something has already broken through — when an [[07_Exiles|exile's]] feelings start to flood the system and the managers couldn't hold the line.

The name says it all. When something feels like it's burning, Firefighters do whatever it takes to put out the fire — immediately, without much regard for side effects.

---

## What Firefighters Do

Firefighter strategies have one goal: stop the pain, fast. They're not planning ahead or considering consequences. They're responding to an emergency.

That emergency response can take many forms:
- Dissociating or checking out mentally
- Rage or explosive anger
- Binge eating, drinking, or substance use
- Compulsive behavior (shopping, sex, gambling, overworking)
- Self-harm
- Oversleeping or complete withdrawal
- Reckless or thrill-seeking behavior
- Flooding with emotion to overwhelm the system

From the outside, firefighter behavior is often the most visible and alarming part of a person's inner system. It's what tends to show up in crisis, in breakdowns, in the behaviors people feel most ashamed of and most desperate to change.

---

## The Shame Trap

One of the cruelest dynamics in IFS is this: firefighter behaviors are often what people feel most ashamed of — and that shame activates more exile pain — which activates more firefighter response. The protection creates the very thing it's trying to escape.

Understanding this loop doesn't excuse behavior. But it fundamentally changes the approach. Trying to force a firefighter to stop without addressing the exile underneath almost never works sustainably. The firefighter isn't being irrational — it's responding to real pain with the only tools it has.

---

## Firefighters and Managers: A Tense Alliance

Managers and Firefighters often don't get along. Managers are horrified by firefighter behavior — it's exactly the kind of chaotic, uncontrolled thing they've been working to prevent. Firefighters don't trust managers to actually handle the pain. They've seen the managers fail.

This creates its own internal conflict: after a firefighter responds, managers often come in hard with criticism, shame, and new rules — which creates more exile activation — which creates more firefighter readiness. The cycle continues.

Working with this dynamic usually involves helping both protector types understand they're protecting the same exile, just at different points in the cycle.

---

## Firefighter Types in the App

The app's assessment identifies 16 distinct firefighter types across four primary orientations:

**Cognitive-primary firefighters** respond through thought — generating constant mental distraction, disconnecting from awareness entirely, or using rationalization to justify emergency behaviors.

**Emotional-primary firefighters** respond through feeling — flooding the system with rage, panic, overwhelming emotion, or dramatic expression that disrupts the system's equilibrium.

**Interoceptive-primary firefighters** respond through the body — creating physical agitation or restlessness, seeking intense sensation, or using physical pain to override emotional pain.

**Impulse-primary firefighters** respond through behavior — substances, food, self-harm, sex, risk, sleep, spending, overwork. The behavior itself is the strategy.

---

## Working with Firefighters

Firefighters are often the most defended parts in the system — not because they're inherently resistant, but because they're used to being condemned. They expect judgment. They often feel they have no choice.

Approaching a firefighter with genuine curiosity — not "why do you do this to me?" but "what are you responding to? what are you trying to protect me from?" — often reveals a part that is terrified of what it's preventing, and exhausted from being on call.

Firefighters generally can't retire until the exile they're protecting has been healed. But once that happens, many firefighters reveal positive qualities — aliveness, passion, spontaneity, intensity — that were being channeled into emergency response and can now be redirected.

---

## Firefighters and the App

- Your identified firefighter parts appear on your [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]], connected to the exiles they protect and the managers they work alongside.
- The [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] feature can begin from a firefighter's activation — following the impulse or behavior back to the exile underneath.
- The [[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]] feature lets you explore a specific firefighter more deeply — its history, its relationship to the exile, what it's really responding to.
- The [[10_Refine|Refine]] feature lets you give a firefighter a name and representation that feels personally accurate.

---

**→ See also:** [[05_Managers]] | [[07_Exiles]] | [[04_How_the_System_Works]]

pages.title: Exiles
pages.content:
# Exiles

**Exiles** are the most vulnerable parts of the inner system. They carry the wounds — the pain, fear, shame, and core beliefs — that the whole protective system formed around. While [[05_Managers|Managers]] and [[06_Firefighters|Firefighters]] do their jobs in the visible foreground of daily life, exiles tend to be hidden, suppressed, and kept out of awareness.

The word "exile" is apt: these parts have been pushed out of the inner community — not out of cruelty, but out of necessity. Their pain was too much for the young system to hold. So the system learned to contain it.

---

## What Exiles Carry

Exiles typically formed during specific experiences — often in childhood — where something overwhelming happened that couldn't be fully processed. This might include:

- Being criticized, shamed, or humiliated
- Loss or abandonment — actual or emotional
- Neglect — not being seen, heard, or adequately cared for
- Feeling helpless or out of control
- Experiences of physical or emotional danger

What makes exiles different from ordinary memories is that they didn't just record what happened — they formed *conclusions* about it. Core beliefs about the self and the world:

- *I am not enough*
- *I am fundamentally broken or defective*
- *I am not lovable as I am*
- *The world is not safe*
- *I will be abandoned if people see the real me*
- *I have to earn love by being useful / perfect / small*

These beliefs are what the protective system is really protecting against — not just the feelings, but the meaning the exile made from its experience.

---

## Exiles Are Young

Most exiles represent younger versions of the self — the part of you that was there when the original wound happened. When an exile gets activated, it can feel like a sudden, jarring regression: you're a grown adult but something inside feels like a terrified child.

This is why exile work requires particular care. The Self needs to be present and resourced enough to be with the exile's pain without becoming overwhelmed by it. The [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In|Self-Energy Check-In]] helps you assess whether you have enough Self present before going into deeper exile territory.

---

## How Exiles Get Activated

Exiles stay hidden most of the time — that's the whole point of the protection system. But certain triggers can bypass the managers and activate exile feelings directly:

- A tone of voice that echoes an early critical figure
- Being ignored in a moment that touches an abandonment wound
- Failing at something in a context that activates shame
- Physical vulnerability — exhaustion, illness, being alone

When this happens, the feeling can seem disproportionate to the situation. That's because it *is* disproportionate to the current situation — the exile is responding to the original wound, which the present situation merely reminded it of.

---

## Exile Types in the App

The app's assessment identifies 14 distinct exile types. Common themes include:

**Abandonment wounds** — Exiles carrying deep fear of being left, unseen, or cut off from connection

**Shame and defectiveness wounds** — Exiles carrying beliefs about being fundamentally flawed, broken, or unworthy

**Helplessness wounds** — Exiles who learned they had no power over what happened to them

**Rejection wounds** — Exiles carrying fear that who they truly are will be unwanted

**Loss wounds** — Exiles still carrying unprocessed grief

Each exile has its own specific character — its age, the experience it formed around, the burden it carries, and what it most needs to hear from the Self.

---

## Working with Exiles

In IFS, exile work is approached carefully and sequentially. The process generally involves:

1. **Getting permission from protectors** — Managers and Firefighters need to feel safe before they'll allow access to the exile. This often means building a working relationship with them first.
2. **Witnessing** — The Self listens to the exile's story. Not trying to fix it, argue with it, or reassure it away — just being present to what it's been carrying.
3. **Unburdening** — Once the exile feels truly seen and heard, the burden (the pain and the beliefs) can begin to lift. This is the core healing moment in IFS.
4. **Integration** — The exile can now update — to know that things are different now, that the Self is here, that it doesn't have to carry the old burden anymore.

The [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] feature helps you find the path to an exile through the protection system. The [[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]] feature supports the witnessing process.

---

## What Happens When Exiles Heal

When an exile's burden is lifted, the whole system shifts. Protectors who've been working to keep that exile contained — sometimes for decades — no longer need to work so hard. Managers soften. Firefighters stand down. The inner system becomes quieter, more spacious, more Self-led.

This is why exile work, while the most delicate part of IFS, is also the most transformative.

---

**→ See also:** [[05_Managers]] | [[06_Firefighters]] | [[04_How_the_System_Works]] | [[12_Trailhead]]

pages.title: Your Parts Map
pages.content:
# Your Parts Map

The **Parts Map** is the visual heart of the app. It's a living diagram of your inner system — showing which parts are active, how they relate to each other, and where your Self sits in relation to all of it.

---

## What You're Looking At

The map uses a **force-directed graph** — nodes for each part, connected by lines that represent relationships. Parts that are closely connected pull toward each other; parts in conflict create tension.

**Nodes (the circles)** represent individual parts. Their size reflects how prominent or active a part is in your system — larger nodes are more dominant. Their color indicates type:

- **Blue** — Manager parts
- **Orange/Red** — Firefighter parts  
- **Purple/Violet** — Exile parts
- **Gold/White** — Self (when shown)

**Lines between nodes** show relationships. Line style carries meaning:
- *Solid lines* — strong, consistent relationships
- *Dashed lines* — occasional or situational connections
- *Line color* — green for harmonious, yellow for neutral, red for conflicting, blue for protective

**Arrows** on protective lines show directionality — which part is protecting which.

---

## Reading Protection Dynamics

The most important relationships on the map are protection relationships — where a Manager or Firefighter connects to an Exile. These are shown with directional arrows pointing from protector to exile.

When you see a cluster of protectors surrounding an exile, you're seeing the architecture of a protection system. The exile is at the center; the protectors orbit it, each doing their part to keep its pain contained.

Understanding *which* parts are protecting *which* exiles is foundational to the deeper work. The [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] feature lets you follow these connections in a guided, step-by-step way.

---

## Reading Part Clusters

Parts that frequently activate together or share a common goal often appear clustered on the map. This might represent:

- **Coalitions** — parts working in alliance (e.g., a Perfectionist and a Critic reinforcing each other)
- **Polarizations** — parts in direct conflict that pull in opposite directions (shown with red tension lines)
- **Protection systems** — a group of protectors organized around a specific exile

The [[04_How_the_System_Works|How the System Works]] page explains these dynamics in more depth.

---

## The Body Map

Alongside the relationship map, the app includes a **body map** — an interactive body silhouette where parts are located according to where you feel them somatically.

Every part has a physical home. Tension in the chest, tightness in the throat, a sinking feeling in the stomach, heat in the face — these are the body's way of signaling that a part is active. The body map makes these locations visible, and you can:

- See at a glance where your system's activity is concentrated
- Tap a body location to see which part lives there
- Track how somatic signatures shift over time

---

## How the Map Is Built

Your map isn't static — it's built from your data and grows as you engage with the app:

- The **[[09_Assessments|Assessment sequence]]** establishes the initial map — which parts are present, how active they are, and their basic relationships
- **[[14_Update|Updates]]** keep the map current as parts shift, insights emerge, and relationships change
- **[[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]]** discoveries add new connections and sometimes new parts
- **[[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]]** sessions deepen part profiles and refine relationship data
- **[[10_Refine|Refine]]** changes how parts appear on the map — custom names, images, prominence

---

## Timeline View

The map also has a **timeline view** — showing how your system has changed over time. You can scroll back to see what your map looked like at any point in your history with the app. This makes your growth visible in a concrete, specific way.

---

## Interacting with the Map

- **Tap any part** to open its profile
- **Long press a connection line** to see relationship details
- **Pinch to zoom** for complex systems
- **Filter the view** by part type, activity level, or context
- **Switch between map view and body map** using the toggle

---

## Working with a Therapist

If you're working with an IFS-trained therapist, your map can be exported and shared through the therapist portal. Your therapist can view your map, annotate it, and use it as a reference in sessions.

---

**→ See also:** [[09_Assessments]] | [[04_How_the_System_Works]] | [[10_Refine]] | [[14_Update]]

pages.title: Assessments
pages.content:
# Assessments

The app uses a **three-tier assessment sequence** to build your initial parts map. Each tier goes deeper than the last — and each one only engages with what the previous tier revealed.

This means the assessment adapts to *your* system rather than asking every possible question to everyone. It's designed to be thorough without being exhausting.

---

## Why Three Tiers?

Your inner system is complex. Trying to map it all at once would be overwhelming — both for you and for the app. The three-tier approach mirrors how good IFS work actually unfolds: start wide, then narrow, then map the relationships.

---

## First Assessment: Parts Identification

**Goal:** Discover which parts are present and active in your system.

The first assessment screens for 44 possible parts — 14 Managers, 16 Firefighters, and 14 Exiles — using 5 questions per part. Questions focus on your actual experience: how often you notice certain thoughts, feelings, behaviors, or physical patterns.

Each part receives a score based on your responses. Parts that score above a threshold are identified as active in your system and carried forward to the second assessment. Parts that don't meet the threshold are set aside — though you can always revisit them manually.

The output of the first assessment is a preliminary list of your active parts, which begins populating your [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]].

---

## Second Assessment: Parts Specifics

**Goal:** Build a detailed profile for each active part.

For every part identified in the first assessment, the second assessment gathers:

- **Triggers** — what situations, people, or internal states activate this part
- **Manifestations** — how it shows up in thoughts, feelings, body sensations, and behaviors
- **Intensity** — how strongly it typically activates
- **Contexts** — where in your life it's most active (work, relationships, alone, etc.)
- **Somatic location** — where you tend to feel it in your body
- **Origins** — any sense of when or how this part first developed

This information becomes the part's profile — visible when you tap it on the [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]], and used by features like [[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]] and [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] to generate personalized content.

---

## Third Assessment: Relationship Mapping

**Goal:** Map how your parts relate to each other.

The third assessment moves from individual parts to the system as a whole. It asks about:

- **Protection relationships** — which Managers and Firefighters protect which Exiles, and how
- **Part-to-part relationships** — alliances, conflicts, and polarizations between parts
- **System-wide patterns** — which parts tend to dominate, which rarely appear, which can't coexist
- **Self access** — how readily you can access Self-energy, and in which contexts

This assessment produces the relational layer of your [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]] — the lines, colors, and directional arrows that show how parts connect.

---

## After the Assessments

The three assessments give the app a working map of your system — detailed enough to be genuinely useful, but not the final word. Your map is a living document.

From this foundation, every other feature in the app builds out and deepens what the assessments established:

- [[10_Refine|Refine]] — personalize your parts beyond the generic descriptions
- [[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]] — go deeper with a specific part
- [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] — follow a protection chain to an exile
- [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In|Self-Energy Check-In]] — track your Self access over time
- [[14_Update|Update]] — log changes, insights, and shifts as they happen

---

## Reassessment

Your system changes over time. Parts become less active; new ones emerge; relationships shift. The app will periodically suggest reassessment — either a full re-run or a targeted update for specific parts or relationships.

You can also manually trigger reassessment for any individual part at any time.

---

**→ Continue to:** [[08_Your_Parts_Map]]
**→ Or explore:** [[10_Refine]] | [[11_Elaboration]] | [[12_Trailhead]]

pages.title: Refine
pages.content:
# Refine

The **Refine** feature is where your parts become truly yours.

The [[09_Assessments|assessments]] give you a working map built from a shared library of parts — accurate in broad strokes, but necessarily generic. The Perfectionist in the app's database and *your* Perfectionist are related, but they're not identical. Your version has a specific character, a specific history, a specific way of speaking.

Refine is the tool that closes that gap.

---

## What You Can Refine

### Rename
Give a part a name that actually fits. Generic names like "Perfectionist" or "Caretaker" are useful for identification — but many people find that a personally meaningful name creates a different quality of relationship with the part.

Some people name parts after their function: "The Overseer," "The Firefighter Who Uses Food." Others name them after people or archetypes the part reminds them of. Others give them entirely personal names. There's no right approach — the question is simply: *what name makes this part more real and recognizable to you?*

You can also note why the name feels right, which becomes part of the part's record.

### Customize Appearance
Assign an image, icon, or color to a part. Visual representation deepens recognition — when you know what your inner critic looks like, you notice it faster. Options include:

- Uploading an image (a photo, artwork, or anything else)
- Choosing from the app's icon library
- Selecting a color that feels right for this part
- Adjusting the part's prominence on the [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]]

### Edit Attributes
Modify or expand the standard attributes the assessment generated. You might find that the default trigger list for your part is partially right but misses something important, or that the description captures the surface but not the texture.

You can also create entirely custom attributes — things that aren't in the standard profile but matter for your specific experience of this part. Examples: "phrases this part uses," "what it looks like when this part is about to activate," "the historical figure it most reminds me of."

### Add Personal Narrative
Document the story of this part — its origin, key moments in your history with it, how it's changed over time. This narrative layer transforms a profile into a relationship — something with depth and history rather than just a set of descriptors.

---

## Why Personalization Matters

IFS works through relationship. The closer your representation of a part is to how it actually appears in your experience, the more readily you'll recognize it when it activates — and the more natural it becomes to relate to it from Self rather than react to it automatically.

Refine isn't about getting the description perfect. It's about building a connection to the part as a specific, real presence in your system — one you know well enough to work with.

---

## When to Refine

Refine is available at any point after the initial assessments — accessible from any part's profile page, from the map, or from the dedicated Refine section.

You don't need to refine all your parts at once. Many people start with the parts that feel most present or most complicated in their lives, then expand from there as their relationship with the system develops.

Refinements are logged as updates in your [[14_Update|Update]] history, so you can track how your understanding of a part has evolved.

---

## Integration with Other Features

- Refined names and images appear on your [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]]
- Elaboration sessions can surface insights that prompt refinement
- After a [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] discovery, newly found parts can be immediately refined
- Refined attributes are used to personalize content throughout the app

---

**→ See also:** [[09_Assessments]] | [[11_Elaboration]] | [[08_Your_Parts_Map]]

pages.title: Elaboration
pages.content:
# Elaboration

**Elaboration** is a guided deep-dive into a single part. Where the [[09_Assessments|assessments]] build a broad map of your system, Elaboration goes vertical — deepening your understanding of one part's story, function, relationships, and needs.

Think of it as sitting down with a part and really listening.

---

## What Elaboration Explores

An Elaboration session works through several dimensions of a part's experience:

**Story and origin** — How did this part develop? What was happening when it first took on this role? What experiences shaped it?

**Appearance and quality** — How does this part present itself? Does it have an image, a voice, a texture, a quality of energy? Getting specific about how a part appears makes it more recognizable.

**Function** — What does this part do? What is it trying to accomplish? What does it believe would happen if it stopped doing its job?

**Fears** — What is this part most afraid of? What is it working to prevent? This question often reveals the exile the part is protecting.

**Needs** — What does this part need from you (from the Self) in order to feel safe enough to relax? Many parts have been doing their job without any acknowledgment for years. Being asked what they need is often surprisingly significant.

**Relationships** — How does this part relate to other parts in the system? Who does it work with? Who does it conflict with? What does it think of the parts it's protecting, or the ones it fights against?

---

## Discovery-First Design

Elaboration begins with experience before explanation. Rather than presenting descriptions and asking you to confirm or deny them, it starts with open-ended prompts that let the part speak for itself:

- *"Bring your attention to this part. What do you notice first — is it a feeling, an image, a sensation, a voice?"*
- *"If this part had something it wanted you to understand about itself, what might that be?"*

Only after initial exploration does the session move into more structured territory. This keeps the process alive rather than clinical.

---

## Elaborating Different Part Types

The session adapts based on part type:

**For Managers:** Elaboration tends to focus on what the manager is controlling, what it fears losing control of, and what it would need to feel safe enough to ease up.

**For Firefighters:** Elaboration focuses on what the firefighter is responding to, what the emergency feels like from its perspective, and what it would need in order to not feel so alone in the crisis.

**For Exiles:** Elaboration follows the witnessing process more closely — listening to the exile's story without trying to change it, acknowledging what it's been carrying, and building the trust needed for deeper healing work.

---

## Pacing and Safety

Elaboration sessions are designed to be emotionally safe. Throughout the process:

- You can pause or exit at any time
- Grounding and regulation tools are accessible with one tap
- The session monitors emotional intensity and can suggest slowing down or shifting focus
- Parts work with exiles includes specific guidance on maintaining adequate Self-energy before going deeper

The [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In|Self-Energy Check-In]] is integrated into Elaboration — particularly when working with exile parts — to ensure you have enough Self present before going into deeper territory.

---

## After Elaboration

The insights from an Elaboration session flow back into the app:

- Part profiles are updated with new information
- New relationship connections are added to the [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]]
- Journal entries are automatically created, capturing key discoveries
- Suggestions may surface for [[10_Refine|Refine]] (if the session revealed something that changes how you'd represent the part) or [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] (if the session pointed toward a protection chain worth following)

---

## Therapist Integration

Elaboration session summaries can be exported for sharing with an IFS therapist. If you're in therapy, Elaboration can serve as between-session preparation — helping you arrive with specific, articulated awareness of a part rather than starting from scratch each session.

---

**→ See also:** [[10_Refine]] | [[12_Trailhead]] | [[07_Exiles]] | [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In]]

pages.title: Trailhead
pages.content:
# Trailhead

A **trailhead** is any inner experience that can serve as an entry point into the system — a thought, a feeling, a body sensation, or an impulse that, when followed with curiosity, leads deeper.

The **Trailhead** feature guides you through that process. Starting with whatever you're noticing right now, it walks you step by step through the protection chain — identifying the parts involved, understanding their relationships, and ultimately discovering the [[07_Exiles|exile]] at the center.

---

## Why Follow a Trail?

Most IFS work begins on the surface. You notice anxiety before a presentation, an urge to eat when you're not hungry, a flash of disproportionate anger. These surface experiences are *symptoms* — signs that a part has activated.

But the part that activated isn't the whole story. It's protecting something. That protection has layers. And without following the trail, you can work on the surface behavior for years without touching what's underneath.

Trailhead is the feature that takes you underneath.

---

## Starting a Trail

You can begin a Trailhead session from several entry points:

- **A thought** — a recurring or intrusive pattern that keeps showing up
- **A feeling** — an emotion that seems larger than the situation warrants
- **A sensation** — a persistent physical experience (chest tightness, a knot in the stomach)
- **An impulse** — a behavioral urge you notice yourself pulled toward
- **A known part** — starting directly from a part already on your map
- **A journal entry or Update** — picking up a thread from something you logged

---

## How a Trail Unfolds

### Step 1: Capture the Entry Point
The app asks you to describe what you're noticing and locate it in your body. Intensity is rated on a 1–10 scale. This initial capture creates an anchor for the session — something specific to return to.

### Step 2: Identify the First Part
Does this experience connect to a part you already know? Or does it point toward something new? If new, you'll name and begin to profile it here.

### Step 3: Self Check-In
Before going deeper, a brief [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In|Self-energy check-in]] ensures you have enough presence to engage with what's ahead. If Self-energy is low, the session will offer grounding support before continuing.

### Step 4: Establish Self-to-Part Connection
The session guides you to create a little space between you and the part — not distance, but differentiation. *"Notice if you can bring curiosity to this part rather than becoming it."* This is the unblending step.

### Step 5: Explore the Part
Questions deepen your understanding of this part's role: What is it trying to do? How long has it been doing this? What would happen if it stopped?

### Step 6: Follow the Trail
*"If you look past this part, what might be underneath? What feelings is it protecting against?"* With permission from the part, the session moves deeper — identifying the next part in the chain and repeating the process.

### Step 7: Exile Discovery
The trail ends when you reach an exile — a part carrying the core pain the protection system organized around. Guidance here is slower and more careful. The session monitors Self-energy closely and supports safe connection with the exile without becoming overwhelmed by its feelings.

### Step 8: Complete and Integrate
A visual map of the complete trail is generated — showing the chain from initial trigger through protectors to the exile. You can name and save the trail, and the system updates your [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]] with any new connections or parts discovered.

---

## Safety Throughout

Trailhead is designed to be emotionally safe without being timid. At every stage:

- A **ground button** is accessible with one tap — interrupting the process and returning you to a regulated state
- The session can be **paused and resumed** at any point
- **Emotional intensity** is monitored, with the session adapting its pace based on what you're experiencing
- **Clear guidance** is provided on what depth is appropriate for solo work versus work with a therapist

The feature includes explicit recommendations for when to bring a trail to a therapist — particularly for trails that reach heavily burdened exiles or touch on experiences of significant trauma.

---

## After a Trail

Trailhead discoveries have cascading effects throughout the app:

- **New parts** discovered during the trail are added to your system
- **New connections** appear on your [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]]
- **Part profiles** are updated with trail-sourced information
- **A journal entry** is automatically created, capturing the trail narrative
- **Suggested next steps** may include [[11_Elaboration|Elaborating]] the discovered exile, scheduling a check-in, or accessing resources related to the exile's specific burden

Your trail is saved in a **Trail Library**, where you can view it, return to it, and track how the protection chain shifts over time.

---

## Therapist Collaboration

Saved trails can be shared with an IFS therapist. Trails are valuable clinical material — they show exactly what protection chain activated around a specific trigger, and what exile it leads to. Bringing a completed trail to a session can significantly accelerate the work.

---

**→ See also:** [[04_How_the_System_Works]] | [[07_Exiles]] | [[11_Elaboration]] | [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In]]

pages.title: Self-Energy Check-In
pages.content:
# Self-Energy Check-In

The **Self-Energy Check-In** is how you track your access to [[03_The_Self|Self]] over time — noticing where you are in any given moment, identifying patterns, and developing a more conscious relationship with what helps and what diminishes your Self presence.

It comes in two forms: a quick daily check-in and a deeper periodic assessment.

---

## Why Track Self-Energy?

Self-energy isn't a permanent state. It fluctuates — with stress, sleep, relationships, life circumstances, and which parts are currently active. Most people have some contexts where Self comes easily (certain relationships, creative activities, time in nature) and others where parts consistently crowd it out (certain work environments, family dynamics, conflict).

Without tracking, this variation stays invisible. You feel the effects — reactive one day, grounded the next — but can't see the pattern. The Check-In makes that pattern visible, which is the first step toward influencing it.

---

## Quick Check-In (Daily)

Designed for regular use — takes 1–2 minutes.

**Overall Self-energy:** A single slider — *"How connected do you feel to your Self-energy right now?"* — from 0% (parts dominate) to 100% (Self clearly present).

**8 C's snapshot:** A rapid 1–5 rating of each Self quality:

| Quality | Description |
|---|---|
| Calm | Centered, settled presence |
| Curious | Open and interested, even toward difficult parts |
| Compassionate | Warmth toward all parts without judgment |
| Clear | Seeing the bigger picture without being caught in it |
| Confident | Trusting your capacity to handle what arises |
| Creative | Flexible, resourceful, able to find new approaches |
| Courageous | Willing to face difficult feelings or parts |
| Connected | In touch with yourself, others, and something larger |

**Blending check:** Which parts feel most active or blended right now? (Select from your identified parts.)

**Context:** Where are you? What's the current situation? This anchors the data to something real.

---

## Comprehensive Assessment (Weekly/Monthly)

A deeper assessment — 5–10 minutes — for understanding patterns rather than just current state.

This assessment expands the 8 C's ratings with specific behavioral indicators, explores how Self-energy varies across different life contexts (work, close relationships, family, social settings, under stress), and maps which parts most commonly blend and what triggers that blending.

It also includes a **Self-leadership assessment** — evaluating your current capacity to notice part activation, maintain Self-to-part differentiation, make decisions from Self rather than from parts, and respond rather than react in triggering situations.

---

## What You'll See Over Time

The check-in builds a **Self-Energy Dashboard** — visualizing your data across days, weeks, and months:

- **Trend lines** showing Self-energy across time
- **Context heatmap** showing which situations correlate with higher and lower Self-energy
- **Blending patterns** — which parts most frequently merge with Self, and in what circumstances
- **Spider chart** of the 8 C's — showing your current profile across all Self qualities, comparable to your personal baseline and previous assessments
- **Self-Parts balance tracker** — a visual representation of how Self-leadership is developing relative to parts activity

---

## Integration Throughout the App

The Self-Energy Check-In doesn't only live in one place — it's woven throughout the app:

- A **quick check-in button** is accessible from the home screen
- [[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]] sessions include a Self check-in before going into deeper protection or exile work
- [[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]] sessions with exiles include Self-energy monitoring throughout
- Low Self-energy readings surface **suggested practices** — grounding exercises, Self-to-part dialogues, somatic anchoring
- The app can send **optional reminders** at user-defined times to prompt a quick check-in

---

## Unblending Tools

When the check-in reveals that a part is strongly blended, the feature offers targeted unblending support:

- **Step-by-step guidance** tailored to the specific part that's blended
- **Grounding techniques** — breathing, somatic anchors, centering practices
- **Self-to-part language prompts** — helping you shift from *"I am anxious"* to *"There's a part of me that feels anxious"*
- **Self Moments Journal** — a space to document moments of clear Self-connection, which can serve as anchors to return to

---

## Self Practices Library

The Check-In feature includes a curated library of practices for strengthening Self-energy access, including:

- Mindfulness and meditation exercises
- Somatic grounding techniques
- Self-to-part dialogue structures
- Compassion practices
- Centering and orientation exercises

Practices are suggested contextually — based on your current check-in data and which Self qualities are currently lower.

---

**→ See also:** [[03_The_Self]] | [[12_Trailhead]] | [[11_Elaboration]] | [[04_How_the_System_Works]]

pages.title: Update
pages.content:
# Update

The **Update** feature is how your map stays alive.

The [[09_Assessments|assessments]] give you a starting map. But inner systems aren't static — parts shift, relationships change, insights emerge, old patterns soften, new ones surface. Without a way to log these changes, your map gradually drifts from your actual current experience.

Update is the tool that keeps everything current.

---

## What Updates Capture

Updates can document almost anything significant in your system:

**Part Activation** — Logging a moment when a specific part activated. When did it happen? What triggered it? How intense was it? How long did it last? What was the physical signature? How did you respond?

**Relationship Change** — Noting a shift in how two parts relate to each other. A polarization that softened, a new alliance you noticed, a protector that relaxed after connecting with the exile it guards.

**System Insight** — Recording a realization or understanding about your system — a pattern you recognized, a connection you made, something that clicked.

**Part Evolution** — Documenting a change in a specific part itself. A critic that's become less harsh. A firefighter that's starting to trust the Self. An exile that's carrying less.

**Progress Milestone** — Marking a significant moment of growth — a moment of genuine Self-leadership in a situation that used to completely flood the system.

---

## Quick vs. Detailed Updates

Updates are designed to fit the moment. Sometimes you have five seconds; sometimes you have five minutes.

**Quick updates** capture the essentials — which part, intensity level, brief context — in a few taps. Use these in the moment, when something activates and you want to mark it before it passes.

**Detailed updates** offer full templates for each update type, with structured prompts and open fields. Use these when you have more time to reflect and want to capture the full texture of what happened.

All updates — quick or detailed — are logged in your history and can be expanded later.

---

## How Updates Feed the Map

Updates aren't just notes. They actively shape your [[08_Your_Parts_Map|Parts Map]] and the rest of the app:

- **Part profiles** are updated with new trigger, manifestation, and context information
- **Relationship lines** on the map update when a relationship change is logged — a conflict softens, an alliance forms, a protection dynamic shifts
- **Part prominence** (node size on the map) adjusts based on recent activation frequency and intensity
- **Timeline view** incorporates updates as markers, so you can see your system's evolution over time

The map at any given moment reflects your most recent understanding — and you can scroll back through history to see how far things have moved.

---

## Patterns and Insights

Over time, your update history becomes a dataset. The app analyzes it to surface:

- **Activation patterns** — which parts activate most, in which contexts, at which times
- **Trigger correlations** — connections between life circumstances and parts activity
- **Sequential patterns** — which parts tend to activate in sequence (a protection chain running in real time)
- **Progress indicators** — measurable shifts in intensity, frequency, and duration of activation over time

These patterns are presented as **weekly insights** and **monthly reviews** — summaries of what's been happening in your system, what's shifting, and what might deserve attention.

---

## Integration with Other Features

Updates are deeply connected to the rest of the app:

- **[[12_Trailhead|Trailhead]]** — updates can identify potential trailhead starting points; trail discoveries are automatically logged as updates
- **[[11_Elaboration|Elaboration]]** — update patterns can surface suggestions for which parts might benefit from elaboration; elaboration insights feed back as updates
- **[[13_Self_Energy_Check_In|Self-Energy Check-In]]** — Self-energy state is optionally recorded with any update, building a correlation picture between activation and Self-access
- **[[15_Couples_Connection|Couples Connection]]** — updates can be shared with a partner (with explicit permission) when they're relevant to relationship dynamics
- **Therapist portal** — updates can be exported for sharing with an IFS therapist, making between-session work visible and continuous

---

## A Note on Consistency

The Update feature is most valuable when used consistently — not perfectly. You don't need to log every activation. Even irregular updates build a picture over time. The app will occasionally prompt you gently after a period of inactivity, but the pace is entirely yours.

Some people update daily; others weekly; others only when something significant happens. All of these approaches produce something useful.

---

**→ See also:** [[08_Your_Parts_Map]] | [[09_Assessments]] | [[13_Self_Energy_Check_In]] | [[12_Trailhead]]

pages.title: Couples Connection
pages.content:
# Couples Connection

**Couples Connection** brings IFS into relationship. It's built on a core insight: most relationship conflict isn't really between two people — it's between two people's *parts*. Understanding that shifts everything.

When your Controlling Manager meets your partner's Withdrawing Manager, the fight looks like it's about the dishes. But underneath, it's a collision of protection systems — each one trying to keep its respective exile safe, neither one trusting the other, both escalating in response to the other's activation.

Couples Connection provides the language, framework, and tools to see this clearly — and to choose a different response.

---

## Core Benefits

- Transforms relationship conflicts from personal attacks into opportunities to understand each other's systems
- Builds a shared language for internal experience (*"my Controlling part is activated"* rather than *"you're making me control everything"*)
- Helps partners recognize when parts — not Self — are driving the interaction
- Teaches partners to become curious about each other's parts rather than reactive
- Supports Self-to-Self connection even during difficult conversations
- Deepens intimacy through genuine understanding of each other's inner worlds

---

## Feature Components

### Couples Assessment and Mapping

Each partner completes their own individual IFS journey through the app. When both partners are using the app, Couples Connection creates a **shared relationship layer** that maps how their systems interact.

The **Relationship Dynamics Assessment** identifies:
- Common interaction patterns and cycles
- Which parts activate in relationship contexts for each partner
- Complementary part pairings (where one partner's part consistently activates a specific part in the other)
- Polarized part pairings across the relationship
- Shared triggers and protection chains

This produces an **Interactive Relationship Map** — showing not just each person's individual system, but the intersections and dynamics between them.

### Check-In System

A **relationship check-in** that both partners can do individually or together:
- Current parts activation status for each partner
- Self-energy level in the relationship right now
- Recent triggers and patterns
- Positive interaction notes

This creates a regular pulse on the relationship — and makes it easier to name what's happening before it escalates.

### Guided Dialogues

Structured frameworks for parts-aware conversations. Templates exist for:

- **Conflict resolution** — slowing down a heated interaction to identify which parts are active
- **Vulnerability sharing** — creating safe conditions for exiles to be witnessed across the relationship
- **Boundary setting** — speaking from Self about needs without activating the other's protectors
- **Intimacy building** — moving from parts-based relating to Self-to-Self connection
- **Healing conversations** — supporting each other through unburdening work

Real-time prompts during conversations support staying in Self — and offer a "pause and reset" pathway when parts take over.

### Healing Dialogue Templates

Specific language structures that help partners communicate from Self:

- *"When your [part] meets my [part], what I experience is..."*
- *"The exile behind my reaction is... and what it needs is..."*
- *"From my Self, I'm curious about your [part]..."*
- *"What my [part] needs you to know is..."*

These aren't scripts to follow robotically. They're training wheels — ways to practice speaking from a different place until it becomes more natural.

---

## Privacy Architecture

Couples Connection is built with privacy as a foundational principle. Each partner's individual IFS work remains fully private unless explicitly shared. The relationship layer only shows:

- What each partner has chosen to share
- Interaction patterns (not the underlying content of private sessions)
- Joint information entered together in couples sessions

Partners can see exactly what is and isn't visible to each other at any time. Sharing is always opt-in and revocable.

---

## Using Couples Connection Without Your Partner

You don't need both partners engaged for Couples Connection to be useful. Working alone, you can:

- Identify which of your parts most commonly activate in the relationship
- Map the interaction pattern from your side
- Practice Self-to-part work on your relationship parts
- Prepare for conversations using the dialogue templates

Relationship healing often starts with one person doing their own work. The tools here support that.

---

## When to Involve a Therapist

Couples Connection is a self-help tool — not a replacement for couples therapy. The app includes clear guidance on when to seek professional support:

- When a specific interaction cycle keeps repeating despite genuine effort
- When exile-level pain is surfacing in the relationship (significant trauma, grief, core shame)
- When one partner is significantly more resistant than the other
- When safety is a concern

The app can help you find an IFS-trained couples therapist and includes an export function for sharing Couples Connection data with a therapist.
